title: From Blocks to Real Code

modules.title: Give the Robot One Instruction
modules.sub: Learn why computers need clear, exact instructions to work
modules.description: Learners step into the role of a game designer giving orders to a robot character, discovering that vague instructions make everything go wrong. They practice writing single, precise commands and see immediately how the robot responds differently based on their wording. This hands-on exploration builds the foundation for understanding what an algorithm really is.

modules.title: What Happens When Steps Are Wrong?
modules.sub: Spot the broken sequence and put the steps back in order
modules.description: Learners are handed a game level where the instructions are jumbled or missing pieces, and they must figure out why everything is broken. By rearranging and correcting the steps, they experience firsthand how order and logic are non-negotiable in code. This module turns mistakes into the lesson, making debugging feel like detective work.

modules.title: Build a Story with Scratch
modules.sub: Drag, snap, and animate your first real interactive story
modules.description: Learners use Scratch's colorful block interface to build a short interactive story set inside their imaginary game studio. They make characters move, speak, and react to clicks by snapping together event and action blocks. By the end, they have a working project they created themselves and can share with others.

modules.title: Your First Real Python Line
modules.sub: Type actual Python code and watch your words come alive
modules.description: Learners make the jump from visual blocks to a real Python editor, typing their very first lines of code including a simple function that takes an input and returns an output. The module uses the game-studio story as context, so writing a function feels like unlocking a new game mechanic rather than a scary leap. Learners run their code, read the result, and tweak it to see what changes.

modules.title: Fix the Bug, Save the Day
modules.sub: Hunt down errors in broken code before the game falls apart
modules.description: Learners are presented with a Python function that is almost right but not quite, and the game studio storyline is on the line until they fix it. They read error messages, form a hypothesis about what went wrong, and make targeted edits to get the code running correctly. This module builds real debugging confidence by making every fix feel like a small victory.

modules.title: Ship Your Mini-Project
modules.sub: Combine everything you know to finish and share your creation
modules.description: Learners bring together their Scratch story and their Python function into one mini-project that represents their personal game studio idea. They make final design decisions, test their project end to end, and write a short explanation of how their code works in their own words. Sharing the finished project with peers closes the loop, celebrating what it means to go from imagination to working creation.

synopsis: You're a game designer whose studio only has one rule: everything you imagine, you have to build yourself — starting with colorful blocks and ending with your first real lines of code. Each level of the story breaks if you don't fix the logic, teaching you to think like a programmer by actually being one.
rationale: Children aged 8-10 have high creative motivation but lack a bridge between visual block-based tools and the text-based coding that powers AI and real software, stalling their progression.
learning_outcome: Students can build a working Scratch project and write and explain a simple Python function that takes an input and returns an output.
source_signals: Show HN: PickCode – An educational coding environment for students after Scratch
